summary: Task 4 implements the InitDiagnostics seam, DiagnosticsState (AtomicReference<InitSnapshot?>), DiagnosticsModule and the NoOpInitDiagnostics test fake exactly per Decisions 6/7, the task spec, and Data Models. Module boundary is clean (zero :app imports in :core-runtime), the Hilt module mirrors LogExportModule's @Binds shape verbatim, and the AtomicReference contract is correct on all six branches including the 'onInitEnd without onInitStart' no-op. No critical or major findings; the items below are minor / nit clarifications.

suggestions.suggestion: concurrentWriterReaderNeverSeesMixedState exercises visibility + reference-swap atomicity (single writer, single reader), which is exactly the prod call pattern (writer on Dispatchers.Default under lifecycleMutex, reader on Main). What it does NOT exercise is the CAS aspect of updateAndGet — i.e. the lost-update scenario that Decision 7 cites as the rationale for AtomicReference over @Volatile. With a single writer the test would still pass if the impl used @Volatile var snapshot: InitSnapshot? (because data-class copy is field-immutable, so `set` of a new reference cannot tear). This is intrinsic to Decision 7's own observation that 'единственный writer ... исключает write-write race по построению'. Not a defect — the test correctly asserts the contract user-spec AC-D1 actually requires (no mixed-field state). Recommendation: add a one-line KDoc on the test method clarifying that this guards visibility + immutable-snapshot atomicity, and that the second-writer / lost-update guarantee from updateAndGet is defence-in-depth not exercised here. No code change needed beyond the comment.
suggestions.benefit: Future maintainers won't be surprised when they realise the test would pass against a @Volatile impl — the comment makes the boundary of what's being tested explicit and matches the spec's own framing.

suggestions.suggestion: Writer thread has no try/catch around the loop body — if writer ever throws (e.g., IndexOutOfBoundsException after a refactor), the AssertionError from the reader will fire spuriously after timing out, masking the real cause. Reader correctly catches its own assertion in readerFailure: AtomicReference<AssertionError?>. Consider adding a symmetric AtomicReference<Throwable?> writerFailure and a try { ... } catch (t: Throwable) { writerFailure.set(t) } around the writer's repeat(iterations) {} block, then re-throw at the end like readerFailure.get()?.let { throw it }. Cheap insurance; mirrors the reader's structure.
suggestions.benefit: Future-proofs the test against silent writer-side regressions and makes failure attribution unambiguous.

suggestions.suggestion: lastInitSnapshot() is a side-effect-free read with no parameters — could be expressed as `val lastInitSnapshot: InitSnapshot? get() = ref.get()` for slightly more idiomatic Kotlin. Current `fun` form is also fine and matches the read-accessor convention used elsewhere (e.g., InitDiagnostics' write methods are functions). No behavioural change either way; flag only because Phase 3.5 Tasks 7/8 will turn this into a hot read site.
suggestions.benefit: Marginally more idiomatic Kotlin; lets call sites read `state.lastInitSnapshot` without parens. Fully optional.

suggestions.suggestion: Two separate methods onInitStart / onInitEnd is the correct shape for the writer call sites in Task 6 (lifecycle is split: snapshot before native init, outcome flip after). Note for future readers that this couples the seam to a fixed start/end protocol — if Phase 4+ ever needs intermediate progress (e.g., 'GPU failed, falling back to CPU' between start and end), the seam will need extension rather than a swap-in. Not actionable today; surfaced because the doc could mention this is intentionally a 2-event protocol, not 1 (atomic write of completed snapshot) or N (progress events).
suggestions.benefit: Documenting the intentional 2-event shape prevents future drift toward N-event progress reporting that doesn't match the AtomicReference single-cell semantics.
